answer:
Debugging Angular Signals Issues

1. Definition & Context

Angular Signals (introduced in Angular 16+) are reactive primitives that track when values change and automatically update dependent computations. Debugging Signals requires understanding their reactivity graph — which signals feed into which computed signals, and when effects re-run. Unlike RxJS Observables, Signals are synchronous and pull-based, which changes debugging patterns.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In Manoj's HR Leave System (Angular 18), the leave balance display used a computed() signal that derived remaining days from totalLeave() minus usedLeave(). After a manager approved a leave, the usedLeave signal was updated via an API response, but the UI sometimes showed the old balance. The issue was that the signal update happened inside a setTimeout callback (from a legacy debounce), which ran outside Angular's change detection zone.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — signal update outside NgZone
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class LeaveBalanceService {
  private _used = signal(0);
  readonly used = this._used.asReadonly();
  readonly remaining = computed(() => this.total() - this._used());

  updateAfterApproval(days: number) {
    // BUG: setTimeout runs outside Angular zone
    setTimeout(() => {
      this._used.update(v => v + days); // Signal updates but UI doesn't refresh
    }, 300);
  }
}

// AFTER — proper zone-aware update
@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class LeaveBalanceService {
  private ngZone = inject(NgZone);
  private _used = signal(0);
  readonly used = this._used.asReadonly();
  readonly remaining = computed(() => this.total() - this._used());

  updateAfterApproval(days: number) {
    // Option 1: Run inside zone
    this.ngZone.run(() => {
      this._used.update(v => v + days);
    });

    // Option 2 (better): Remove setTimeout, use RxJS debounce
    // this.approvalSubject.pipe(debounceTime(300)).subscribe(...)
  }
}

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj noticed the leave balance widget froze after approval. He added effect(() => console.log('remaining:', this.remaining())) — it logged correctly inside the effect but the template didn't update. This confirmed the signal value was right but change detection wasn't running. He traced the set() call and found it inside a setTimeout from an old debounce pattern. Replacing it with RxJS debounceTime fixed the issue since RxJS operators run inside NgZone by default.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Signal updates outside NgZone don't trigger CDUse RxJS operators instead of setTimeout/setInterval; or wrap in NgZone.run()
Computed signals accessing signals conditionally — dependency not trackedEnsure all signal reads happen unconditionally in computed body; use early returns after all reads
Effect cleanup not handled — memory leaksUse effect() with onCleanup callback; destroy effects in ngOnDestroy

7. Validation Strategy

  • Unit test: Update signal, assert computed value reflects change synchronously
  • Integration test: Call API → update signal → verify template binding shows new value
  • Effect test: Use TestBed.flushEffects() to verify effects run after signal changes

Comparing Angular Signals Approaches

1. Definition & Context

The two approaches compared are Signals-first (using signals, computed, and effects as the primary state management) and RxJS-first with Signal interop (keeping RxJS Observables as the core and using toSignal()/toObservable() for template binding). The choice depends on the team's RxJS proficiency and the complexity of async data flows.

2. Real Project Use-Case — HR Leave Management System

In the HR Leave System, Manoj used a hybrid approach: RxJS for HTTP calls and complex async flows (Socket.io events, approval chain polling), and Signals for local component state (form validity, UI toggles, derived computations like leave balance). The toSignal() bridge converted HTTP observables to signals for template binding, eliminating async pipe boilerplate while keeping RxJS's powerful operators for data transformation.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// Hybrid approach in HR Leave System
@Component({ selector: 'app-leave-dashboard' })
export class LeaveDashboardComponent {
  private http = inject(HttpClient);
  private destroy$ = new Subject();

  // Signal for local UI state
  selectedTab = signal<'pending' | 'approved' | 'all'>('pending');
  isLoading = signal(false);

  // RxJS for HTTP → toSignal for template
  private leaves$ = this.http.get('/api/leave/my-leaves');
  leaves = toSignal(this.leaves$, { initialValue: [] });

  // Computed from both signal and toSignal
  filteredLeaves = computed(() => {
    const tab = this.selectedTab();
    const all = this.leaves();
    return tab === 'all' ? all : all.filter(l => l.status === tab);
  });

  pendingCount = computed(() =>
    this.leaves().filter(l => l.status === 'pending').length
  );
}

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj initially used toSignal() without an initialValue, which threw NG0950: toSignal() requires an initial value at runtime. He added { initialValue: [] } to fix it. Later, he discovered that toSignal() automatically subscribes and unsubscribes — removing the need for manual takeUntilDestroyed() on those observables.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
toSignal() without initialValue throws at runtimeAlways provide { initialValue } or use { requireSync: true } with synchronous observables
Mixing signals and observables creates confusion about data flowEstablish team convention: Signals for sync local state, RxJS for async; document the boundary
Overusing effect() for side effects that should be RxJS streamsUse effect() only for DOM side effects; use RxJS tap() for data-flow side effects

7. Validation Strategy

  • Template test: Change selectedTab signal, verify filteredLeaves computed re-evaluates and template updates
  • Memory test: Navigate away from component, verify toSignal() subscriptions are cleaned up (no RxJS leak)
  • Interop test: Use toObservable(selectedTab) to verify signal changes emit on the observable stream

Debugging Angular Change Detection Issues

1. Definition & Context

Angular's Change Detection (CD) walks the component tree to check if template bindings need updating. Debugging CD issues involves identifying why a component isn't updating (missed trigger) or why it's updating too often (performance drag). With OnPush strategy, CD only runs when inputs change by reference, events fire, or markForCheck() is called — requiring more explicit control.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In the CRM Dashboard, Manoj used OnPush on all components for performance. The pipeline chart component received deal data via @Input() deals: Deal[]. When a deal's stage was updated in-place (mutating the array), the chart didn't re-render because OnPush checks reference equality — the array reference hadn't changed.

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// BEFORE — mutation doesn't trigger OnPush
updateDealStage(dealId: string, newStage: string) {
  const deal = this.deals.find(d => d.id === dealId);
  if (deal) deal.stage = newStage; // Mutation! Same array ref
  // OnPush child won't detect this change
}

// AFTER — immutable update creates new reference
updateDealStage(dealId: string, newStage: string) {
  this.deals = this.deals.map(d =>
    d.id === dealId ? { ...d, stage: newStage } : d
  ); // New array ref → OnPush detects change
}

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj used Angular DevTools' profiler to check CD cycles. The pipeline chart component showed 0 CD runs after the deal update, confirming OnPush was blocking the update. He traced the parent component and found the array mutation. The fix was adopting immutable patterns throughout — using map() and spread operators instead of direct property assignment.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Mutating @Input arrays/objects with OnPushAlways create new references: [...arr], {...obj}, or use Immer for complex mutations
Overusing markForCheck() as a workaroundFix the root cause (mutation) instead of forcing CD; markForCheck() is a bandaid
Default CD on root component causes unnecessary checks on entire treeUse OnPush on ALL components; add it to Angular schematic defaults

7. Validation Strategy

  • Angular DevTools profiler: Verify CD runs only when expected after each interaction
  • OnPush audit: Script checking all components use changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush
  • Immutability test: ESLint rule no-param-reassign to prevent input mutations

Comparing Angular Change Detection Approaches

1. Definition & Context

The two approaches are Default Change Detection (checks every component on every event) and OnPush Change Detection (only checks when input references change, events fire in the component, or explicit markForCheck()). Default is simpler but slower for large apps; OnPush requires immutable data patterns but dramatically reduces CD cycles.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard initially used Default CD. With 200+ deals rendered across pipeline columns, filter dropdowns, and activity feeds, every click triggered CD on ALL components — even static headers and footers. Angular DevTools showed 800+ CD checks per interaction. After switching to OnPush on all 30+ components, CD checks dropped to 15-20 per interaction, and the Lighthouse performance score improved from 65 to 92.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// angular.json — make OnPush the default for all new components
{
  "schematics": {
    "@schematics/angular:component": {
      "changeDetection": "OnPush"
    }
  }
}

// Component with OnPush
@Component({
  selector: 'app-deal-card',
  changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush,
  template: `
{{ deal().title }} - {{ deal().stage }}
`
})
export class DealCardComponent {
  deal = input.required(); // Signal-based input (Angular 17+)
}

5. Debugging Narrative

After migrating to OnPush, Manoj found one component that broke: the notification badge in the header. It subscribed to Socket.io events and updated a counter, but the counter didn't reflect in the template. The fix was adding ChangeDetectorRef.markForCheck() inside the Socket.io callback since the event came from outside Angular's zone.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
External events (WebSocket, timers) don't trigger OnPushUse markForCheck() in callbacks or convert to RxJS observables processed inside NgZone
Forgetting to create new references for nested objectsUse spread operators consistently; consider Immer's produce() for complex nested updates
Gradual migration leaves some components on Default, negating benefitsMigrate all at once; use ESLint rule to enforce OnPush on every component

7. Validation Strategy

  • Performance profiling: Compare CD cycles before/after migration using Angular DevTools
  • Lighthouse audit: Verify performance score improvement (target: 90+)
  • CI check: Lint rule ensuring all components use OnPush

Debugging Component Composition Issues

1. Definition & Context

Component Composition is the pattern of building complex UIs by combining small, focused components through inputs, outputs, content projection (ng-content/children), and slots. Debugging composition issues involves tracing data flow through component boundaries, identifying broken contracts between parent and child, and understanding content projection mechanics.

2. Real Project Use-Case — CRM Sales Dashboard

In the CRM Dashboard, Manoj built a reusable <data-card> component with header, body, and footer slots via ng-content select. When the sales pipeline page used it, the footer content (action buttons) appeared in the body slot instead. The issue: the CSS class used in ng-content select=".card-footer" didn't match the actual class on the projected content (card-actions was used instead).

3. Diagnostic Flow Diagram

4. Debugging Code

// data-card.component.html — Reusable card with named slots


  

    
  

  

     
  

  

    
  




// Usage — attribute selectors are more reliable than class selectors

  
Pipeline Summary

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj initially used CSS class selectors (.card-footer) for ng-content, which was fragile — class names could be overridden by Tailwind utilities or removed during refactoring. He switched to attribute selectors ([card-footer]) which are explicit, don't conflict with styling classes, and are self-documenting in the template.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
CSS class-based ng-content selectors break when classes changeUse attribute selectors: select="[card-footer]" instead of select=".card-footer"
Unmatched projected content falls into default slot silentlyAdd a fallback message in default slot; log warnings in dev mode when unexpected content appears
Deeply nested composition makes data flow hard to traceLimit nesting to 3 levels; use a state management pattern for data that needs to cross many boundaries

7. Validation Strategy

  • Slot test: Render component with content in each slot, verify DOM structure matches expected layout
  • Missing slot test: Render without footer content, verify empty footer section doesn't break layout
  • Storybook: Document all slot combinations in Storybook stories for visual verification

Comparing Component Composition Approaches

1. Definition & Context

The two approaches are Content Projection (Slots) — parent provides content that the child renders in designated areas via ng-content (Angular) or children/slots (React) — and Render Props / Template Refs — parent provides a template/function that the child calls with data, enabling the child to control what's rendered while the parent controls how. Slots are simpler for static layout customization; render props give the child more control over data-driven rendering.

2. Real Project Use-Case — ContactsHub

In ContactsHub (React 18), Manoj built a reusable <DataTable> component. He initially used children composition (passing <Column> components), but needed the table to pass row data to each cell for custom rendering. He switched to a render prop pattern where columns defined a render function: render: (contact) => <Avatar src={contact.avatar} />. This gave maximum flexibility while keeping the table logic (sorting, pagination) centralized.

3. Comparison Diagram

4. Implementation Code

// React: Render prop pattern for DataTable (ContactsHub)
interface Column {
  key: string;
  header: string;
  render: (item: T) => React.ReactNode;
  sortable?: boolean;
}

function DataTable({ data, columns }: { data: T[]; columns: Column[] }) {
  return (
    {columns.map(c => )}
        {data.map((item, i) => (
          
            {columns.map(c => )}
          
        ))}
      

      
        
{c.header}

      
      
{c.render(item)}

    

  );
}

// Usage
  },
    { key: 'name', header: 'Name', render: (c) => {c.name} },
    { key: 'email', header: 'Email', render: (c) => {c.email} },
  ]}
/>

5. Debugging Narrative

Manoj initially passed columns as children (<Column render={...} />), but React's Children.map couldn't easily extract props from child elements for sorting logic. Switching to a columns prop array gave the DataTable direct access to column definitions, making sorting, filtering, and resize implementation straightforward.

6. Pitfall & Mitigation

PitfallMitigation
Render prop functions re-created every render causing unnecessary re-rendersMemoize render functions with useCallback or define them outside the component
Complex render props create deeply nested JSX ("callback hell")Extract complex renderers into named components; limit render prop to simple expressions
Type safety lost when using generic render propsUse TypeScript generics on the component: DataTable<Contact> ensures render function receives correct type

7. Validation Strategy

  • Render test: Pass mock data and columns, verify each cell renders the custom content
  • Type test: Verify TypeScript catches type errors when render function accesses wrong property
  • Performance test: Render 1000 rows, verify no unnecessary re-renders using React DevTools Profiler
